metadata.title: Talking to Strangers: What We Should Know About the People We Don't Know — Book Review

metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Skilled, competent people still misjudged strangers, so the failure isn't incompetence. Cases with hundreds of accusers aren't simple narratives of predation once you look closely. Transparency is a culturally specific illusion, not a reliable human universal. What one culture reads as fear another reads as threat, and the mismatch misleads us.

artifactLines: Skilled, competent people still misjudged strangers, so the failure isn't incompetence.
Cases with hundreds of accusers aren't simple narratives of predation once you look closely.
Transparency is a culturally specific illusion, not a reliable human universal.
What one culture reads as fear another reads as threat, and the mismatch misleads us.

beats.narration_text: Gladwell's claim that transparency is a cultural illusion rather than a human universal is the part I want to pressure-test. Help me go deeper on the mismatch that makes us misread strangers. Show me: (1) exactly how the transparency assumption fails when someone's demeanor doesn't match their intent, (2) a concrete case where Americans read fear as threat and got it catastrophically wrong, and (3) one practical adjustment I could make in a high-stakes conversation to stop over-trusting my own read of a stranger.
